query: bone removal methods during periradicular surgery

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the physical characteristics of the root fragment to be removed, a surgeon may attempt all three of these principles - often simultaneously - until the root mobilizes. With time, the surgeon can predictably retrieve the root with forceps. A periotome is a handy instrument to use when desiring to preserve gingival and alveolar architecture. Manual and powered versions are available. The periotome uses a thin blade-like working end wedged within the periodontal ligament space, severing the ligamentous attachment encircling the root. The periotome avoids the need for excessive cortical bone removal. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The surgical handpiece used for bone removal must exhaust air pressure away from the surgical site to avoid air embolism or tissue emphysema. Lingual bone removal must be avoided to prevent injury to the lingual nerve when extracting mandibular teeth. A common practice after exposing the tooth is to section it to facilitate removal. The tooth is typically sectioned 3/4 of the way toward the lingual aspect using the surgical handpiece and bur, and the remaining union is split using an elevator. The surgical bur is placed parallel to the buccal groove in impacted molars.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Instruments -- Function -- Surgical Straight Handpiece
retrieved_docs.content: Most commonly, round burs are used to remove the alveolar bone. During tooth removal, surgeons may use a fissure bur for crown and root sectioning. [26] [27] Fissure burs are also used for coronectomy procedures, which involve removing the crown of the tooth and reducing the remaining roots 3mm below the crest of the buccal and lingual plates. [28] Bone rongeurs are instruments that are also used to aid bone removal. They can remove bony prominences or irregular ridges, which can be smoothed using a bone file, allowing for improved healing. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The Benex device (Benex, Lucerne, Switzerland) is the most frequently cited vertical extraction system on the market today. However, some notable disadvantages of the Benex system are that the technique is time-consuming, and its mechanism is achievable only by counterbalancing against the dental arch, placing adjacent teeth and alveolar bone at risk for accidental fracture. [25] Additionally, the system is costly when compared to more conventional armamentaria. For these reasons, vertical extraction systems may not be the most practical means for the atraumatic extraction of teeth and roots. On the contrary, some have suggested a more cost-effective alternative to the Benex extraction system using a similar variation of the vertical extraction principle used by Benex. The technique requires using a miniplate screw fixated within the radicular canal and with a wire wound under the head of the screw. The wire-screw-root combination extrudes coronally using a wire twister and the nearby palatal bone as the fulcrum. [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Equipment -- Surgical Handpiece and Burs
retrieved_docs.content: The two main types of surgical burs are fissure burs and round burs. Round burs are often used for buccal bone removal as they do not create sharp edges. Fissure burs can also be used for bone removal and for sectioning of crowns and roots.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: If the tooth is not visualized upon flap reflection, a surgical handpiece with a round or straight tapered fissure bur should be used to remove bone, uncover the tooth, and create a trough around the crown of the tooth to the level of the cementoenamel junction. When using the surgical handpiece, care should be taken not to damage neighboring tooth roots or, in the 3rd molar site, the lingual plate, as the lingual nerve may be in direct contact with the lingual plate in this area. Copious irrigation is also needed when using the surgical handpiece to prevent overheating the bone and removal of debris in the surgical site, as temperatures above 47 °C could cause irreversible osteonecrosis. [34] At this point, a surgical elevator may be used to attempt delivery of the tooth. However, the adjacent teeth must not be used as a fulcrum to prevent damage to these teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Instruments -- Function -- Periotomes and Luxators
retrieved_docs.content: Where the root apex fractures within the socket, a root pick elevator is useful in luxating the apical tip. Where the surgeon struggles to find a definitive application point, bone removal at the level of the apical root tip may be required to improve access. These fine instruments have a posterior curve of the working end, allowing for successful application and luxation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Retained dental roots may present with one or many indications for removal. Some of the most common indications for the extraction of root fragments are: The presence of acute or chronic infection Partial or complete fracture of the crown Future dental implant placement Pain Vertical root fractures Periodontal disease Caries Presence of pathology Future prosthetic restoration of the site with a fixed or removable prosthesis Preservation of vital adjacent teeth and structures Patient esthetic concerns

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of dental roots requires many of the same surgical instruments in conventional exodontia. The typical surgical setup usually includes a combination of the following: Elevators Luxators Forceps Root tip picks Retractors Curets Dental explorers Dental mirrors Scissors Scalpels Hemostats Periodontal Probes Rongeurs Bone files Local anesthetic needles and syringes Irrigation needles and syringes Local anesthetic Sterile gauze Sutures Surgical handpiece Surgical bur Sterile saline Sterile water Bite block

retrieved_docs.title: Pericoronitis -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Treatment Options
retrieved_docs.content: Soft tissue surgery: Removing the infected soft tissue can help resolve pericoronitis, as it is the soft tissue disease overlying an erupting third molar. The operculum, the soft tissue covering the erupting third molar, can be removed to eliminate the deep pocket between the gingiva and the tooth. This surgery is also known as operculectomy, and laser, electrocautery, radiofrequency ablation, or scalpel can be used. Removal of the soft tissue covering the third molar can also help with tooth eruption. [10] This treatment option is limited to third molars with a favorable eruption position. For example, if a tooth is erupting vertically with adequate space from the distal aspect of the tooth to the anterior border of the ramus, operculectomy can help resolve pericoronitis. However, if a tooth is horizontally impacted or there is inadequate space for eruption, the areas that are hard to clean likely persist, which may cause pericoronitis to recur. Pericoronal ostectomy: If a tooth is in a favorable position to erupt into functional occlusion with adequate space, removing the bone covering the coronal portion of the erupting tooth can help accelerate the eruption. [11] Extractions: Tooth extraction is an irreversible procedure. Dental providers should consider other treatment options and compare the risks and benefits before determining whether a tooth should be removed. Also, a referral to a specialist may be needed depending on the clinician’s experience or available surgical equipment. If, after close examination and assessment, a tooth is deemed not likely to erupt into functional occlusion and has an increased risk of persistent infection, extraction of the tooth or the opposing tooth can be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Once consent is obtained and all team members agree with the planned procedure, the patient is locally anesthetized, and the anesthetic is allowed time to take effect before initiating surgery. There is much data to support atraumatic teeth extraction whenever feasible. With the advent of new materials and technology, today’s surgeon is responsible for knowing how to extract teeth in a way that facilitates optimum preservation of bone and soft tissue. However, it is essential to note that no extraction is entirely devoid of trauma, and all forms of tooth loss result in some alveolar bone resorption. [19] There are a variety of methods at the surgeon’s disposal uniquely focused on the removal of root segments:

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Another modern advancement in atraumatic tooth extraction is Piezosurgery. This cutting system creates osteotomies through ultrasonic vibrations, which promotes cleaner surgical cuts compared to oscillating saws or surgical burs. Piezosurgery permits the surgeon to remove only the necessary bone to facilitate root extraction. It also does not cause harm to soft tissues or neurovascular structures, unlike surgical burs or oscillating saws that obliterate hard and soft tissue indiscriminately. [20] Surgical skill comes with knowledge and consistent practice. As the surgeon gains experience in exodontia, they will be able to feel when their application of force is appropriate, promoting efficiency and safety during the surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Technique or Treatment -- Surgical Removal
retrieved_docs.content: The socket should be irrigated and carefully debrided to sound bone, avoiding the apical aspect of the socket to prevent damage to the inferior alveolar nerve. Closure of the socket is typically achieved with resorbable sutures.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Retained dental roots arise most commonly due to prior dental extraction procedures, during which they are intentionally or unintentionally left behind. They can also arise from facial trauma that results in dental fractures. Their removal may be warranted to make way for a future dental implant or dental prosthesis or due to odontogenic pain, infection, or associated pathology. The extraction of retained root tips is a surgical procedure frequently performed in the outpatient setting by dentists and dental specialists. Appropriately managing retained roots requires knowledge of dental morphology, current root removal techniques, indications, and contraindications. This activity reviews the proper evaluation and treatment strategies used to extract dental roots. Additionally, it highlights the roles and responsibilities of healthcare team members when performing this type of surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Mitigation of surgical risk occurs through proper planning and good technique, but untoward events can still manifest intraoperatively or during the postoperative period. Most are not unique to root extractions and are just as likely to present in simple or surgical exodontia cases. Complications of all tooth extractions include: Pain Bleeding Infection Fracture of bone or jaws Osteomyelitis Osteonecrosis Alveolar osteitis Prosthetic defects Impaired wound healing Burns [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Indications -- Retained Dental Roots
retrieved_docs.content: Retained dental roots can be present after crown fractures due to caries or trauma or following incomplete dental extraction. Some situations will allow for the retention of a dental root, such as for prosthetic reasons or due to the risk of damaging surrounding structures in an attempt to retrieve them. [16] However, they have the potential to cause infection and pain and are thus commonly advised for pre-emptive removal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2430
retrieved_docs.content: Other miscellaneous procedures are classified as a subset of other nonoperative procedures, indicating that they fall under the broader category of nonoperative interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2549
retrieved_docs.content: Ablation is categorized as one of the “other or therapeutic procedures on bone” and is also identified by the Arabic term استئصال مشط القدم, indicating its role in bone‑removal interventions. Similarly, Excision of sequestrum of metarsal bone is classified as an “other or therapeutic procedure on bone” and is likewise referred to as استئصال مشط القدم. Both Ablation and Excision of sequestrum of metarsal bone therefore belong to the same therapeutic bone‑procedure group, emphasizing their shared purpose of removing diseased or unwanted bone tissue, particularly of the metatarsal region. This unified classification aids clinicians and coders in recognizing these interventions as related bone‑surgical techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1765
retrieved_docs.content: Craterization of bone and sequestrectomy of tarsals or metatarsals are both categorized as other or therapeutic procedures on bone, indicating that they are surgical interventions involving bone tissue. Craterization of bone refers to a technique that creates a crater‑like defect in bone for therapeutic purposes, while sequestrectomy of tarsals or metatarsals involves the removal of necrotic bone fragments from the foot’s tarsal or metatarsal bones. Both procedures are employed in orthopedic and surgical practice to treat bone pathology and promote healing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1930
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system” functions as a classification label for the broader category “other or therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system.” Within this classified group, the specific intervention “Reopening of osteotomy site of facial bone” is regarded as a related and possibly synonymous procedure, indicating that it belongs to the same set of musculoskeletal therapeutic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1904
retrieved_docs.content: Sequestrectomy of phalanges of foot is a surgical procedure that involves the direct removal of a sequestrum, which is a piece of devitalized bone, from the phalanges of the foot. The direct morphological target of this operation is the sequestrum itself. The excision of sequestrum of phalanx is recognized as a specific form of sequestrectomy of phalanges of foot, representing a more detailed description of the same underlying surgical concept. Together, these terms describe the same clinical intervention focused on eliminating dead bone tissue from foot phalanges to promote healing and prevent infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1794
retrieved_docs.content: Sequestrectomy of tarsals or metatarsals is classified as an other or therapeutic procedure on bone. A sequestrum, which is a fragment of necrotic bone, is directly related to the morphology of other or therapeutic procedures on bone, indicating that the management of sequestra often involves bone‑directed therapeutic interventions such as sequestrectomy. Recognizing that sequestrectomy of the tarsal or metatarsal bones falls within this broader category of bone therapeutic procedures helps clinicians select appropriate surgical strategies for removing sequestra and preserving foot function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1820
retrieved_docs.content: Excision of sequestrum of phalanx is classified as an other or therapeutic procedure on bone, representing a surgical approach to remove a sequestrum—a piece of devitalized bone that is directly related to bone pathology. The entity Sequestrum itself is described as a direct morphology of other or therapeutic procedures on bone, highlighting its role as a characteristic morphological finding that guides bone‑related interventions. Sequestrectomy of phalanges of foot is a specific form of excision of sequestrum of phalanx; it is a direct morphology of Sequestrum, indicating that this procedure directly addresses the sequestrum present in the foot phalanges. Together, these concepts delineate the hierarchical and morphological relationships among bone‑targeted therapeutic procedures, the pathological entity Sequestrum, and the specific surgical technique of sequestrectomy in the phalanges.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2477
retrieved_docs.content: Sequestrectomy of tarsals or metatarsals is classified as an other or therapeutic procedure on bone, involving the surgical removal of necrotic bone tissue from the tarsal or metatarsal bones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 675
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on bone is the classification category that includes the surgical intervention known as total ostectomy of tarsals and metatarsals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 780
retrieved_docs.content: Craterization of bone, Excision of sequestrum of phalanx, Sequestrum, Partial excision of phalanx of toe (procedure), Sequestrectomy of phalanges of foot and the category other or therapeutic procedures on bone are closely interconnected concepts in orthopedic surgery. Craterization of bone is related to the broader group of other or therapeutic procedures on bone and is specifically linked to the partial excision of a toe phalanx, indicating its use in targeted bone removal. Excision of sequestrum of phalanx is classified as an other or therapeutic procedure on bone and functions as a general category that encompasses the more specific sequestrectomy of phalanges of foot. Sequestrectomy of phalanges of foot directly addresses a sequestrum, a fragment of necrotic bone, representing a direct morphological intervention on the sequestrum. Together, these procedures illustrate the therapeutic approach to managing sequestra in the phalanges of the foot and toe, emphasizing the removal of dead bone to promote healing and restore function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2204
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system is a classification term that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system, grouping together various interventions aimed at treating musculoskeletal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 218
retrieved_docs.content: operative options. Bone necrosis (النخرُ العظمي) is positioned within the Mitchell‑type bone‑cutting taxonomy, with التهاب مخ العظام (osteomyelitis) as a member, illustrating how pathological entities are integrated into procedural classification systems that guide diagnosis and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1793
retrieved_docs.content: Excision of sequestrum of phalanx is a general surgical approach for removing dead bone tissue (sequestrum) from a phalanx, and Sequestrectomy of phalanges of foot represents a specific application of this technique focused on the foot’s toe bones, wherein the sequestrated portion of the phalanges is precisely excised. This relationship indicates that the foot‑specific sequestrectomy is a subset of the broader excision of sequestrum procedure.
